mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover Sport 4.4 SDV8 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 448DT V8 diesel typically costs £6,000–£9,000 supply only, with fitting adding £1,600–£2,200. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Range Rover Sport.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover Sport 4.4 SDV8 (448DT) engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the 448DT is widely considered the most reliable of the JLR V8 diesels. It has no catastrophic design flaws like the crank bearing issues on the 306DT. The main watchpoints are oil cooler leaks and DPF regeneration issues — both are manageable with preventative maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Range Rover Sport 4.4 SDV8 engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil cooler failure (oil and coolant mixing), DPF regeneration issues (short journey use), and coolant crossover pipe leaks. The engine itself is robust and not prone to bottom-end failure.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover Sport 4.4 SDV8 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. An L494 Range Rover Sport 4.4 SDV8 is a £15,000–£40,000 vehicle — a £7,500 recon is 19–50% of the car's value. This is within our 60% Land Rover scrap threshold for mid-to-high-value examples; lower-value examples (sub-£18,000) may require careful evaluation.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover Sport 4.4 SDV8 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 4.4 SDV8 engine code is 448DT — a 4.4-litre V8 diesel. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the oil cooler failure on a 4.4 SDV8?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — many specialists recommend replacing the oil cooler preventatively at 70,000–100,000 miles. If you notice coolant loss or milky oil, have the oil cooler inspected immediately. Catching it early prevents oil/coolant contamination from causing bearing damage.

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Range Rover Sport 4.4 SDV8 engine typically takes 18–24 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover Sport 4.4 SDV8 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Oil Cooler Leaks (Valley of the V8)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, oil in coolant (or vice versa), engine overheating, milky residue on oil filler cap.
problems.repairCost: £1,200–£2,500 (oil cooler replacement) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before contamination causes bearing damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant levels drop or oil appears milky

problems.recommendation: The 448DT's oil cooler sits in the valley of the V8 — a labour-intensive location. When the cooler fails, oil and coolant can mix, potentially causing bearing damage. If you notice unexplained coolant loss, overheating, or milky oil, have the oil cooler inspected immediately. Replacing it preventatively at 70,000–100,000 miles is strongly recommended by specialists.

problems.title: DPF Regeneration Issues (Short Journey Use)
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, reduced power, increased fuel consumption, automatic stop-start unavailable, engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (DPF clean or forced regeneration) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.replacementCost: £1,500–£3,000 (DPF replacement, if physically damaged) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.recommendation: Like all modern diesels, the 448DT needs regular long runs to regenerate the DPF. If your Sport 4.4 SDV8 is used primarily for short journeys, plan a 30–45 minute motorway run every two weeks to allow passive regeneration. If the DPF warning light comes on, book a forced regeneration promptly.

problems.title: Coolant Crossover Pipe Leaks
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, low coolant warning light, occasional burning smell, overheating.
problems.recommendation: The plastic coolant crossover pipes on the 448DT are a known weak point — they can become brittle and crack over time. If you notice coolant loss with no visible external leak, have the cooling system pressure-tested. Replacing the pipes preventatively every 80,000 miles is cheaper than the overheating damage they can cause.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover Sport 4.4 SDV8 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it before oil/coolant contamination causes bearing damage
Routine maintenance — forced regen or clean
Viable on lower-mileage examples; assess overall condition
Little left to salvage — replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Range Rover Sport 4.4 SDV8? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented oil cooler service history (replaced preventatively at 70,000–100,000 miles)
* No signs of oil and coolant mixing (milky oil, coolant loss)
* Regular long runs (DPF health) — or proof of forced regenerations
* Full JLR service history with correct oil spec
avoidIf: * Coolant loss with no diagnosis — oil cooler or crossover pipes may be failing
* Milky residue on oil filler cap — sign of oil cooler failure
* DPF warning light with no regeneration history — may indicate heavy clogging
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — potentially expensive emissions system issues

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 448DT is the most reliable of the JLR V8 diesels — no catastrophic bottom-end design flaws, no crank bearing fatigue, and a generally robust engineering base. The oil cooler and DPF regeneration are the two watchpoints, both manageable with preventative maintenance. A well-maintained 4.4 SDV8 is a durable, torque-rich flagship powertrain.

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover Sport 4.4 SDV8 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Sport 4.4 SDV8 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Oil Check — Look for milky residue on oil filler cap (oil cooler failure indicator)
* Coolant Level — Check for unexplained loss; oil cooler or crossover pipes may be leaking
* Cold Start — Listen for smooth start; no chain rattle on this engine
* Service History — including engine era (448DT V8) and confirmed oil change history
* DPF Status — Check for warning lights; short journeys can cause clogging
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved emissions issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
